title: Two Ways to Recommend: Content-Based vs Collaborative
concept: Recommendation engines split into two fundamentally different approaches: content-based filtering compares item attributes to what you already liked, while collaborative filtering compares your behavior to other users' behavior — no item attributes required.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In anomaly detection, why can a model flag a transaction as suspicious without ever being shown a labeled example of fraud?

options.text: It learns what "normal" looks like from the data, then flags anything that breaks that pattern
It downloads a list of known fraud cases from the internet
It asks a human to confirm every single transaction first

exercises.explain: Exactly! Anomaly detection models learn the normal pattern and flag deviations — no labeled "fraud" examples required. Now let's look at a different kind of pattern-finding: recommending what you'll like next.

cards.title: Two engineers, two strategies
cards.body: Two engineers are each asked to build "You might also like" for a movie app. One says, "Let's look at what's IN the movie — genre, actors, runtime." The other says, "Let's look at WHO liked the movie — and what else those people watched." Neither is wrong. They're building two completely different kinds of recommendation engines.

cards.title: Content-based filtering
cards.body: Content-based filtering recommends items whose own attributes are similar to items you already liked — matching genre, tags, actors, or other item features. It only ever looks at the items themselves, never at other users.

cards.title: A friend who never reads labels
cards.body: Content-based filtering is like a clerk who studies every product's label — "100% cotton, blue, size M" — and finds you something with a matching label. Collaborative filtering is the opposite: a friend who's never read a single label in their life. They just remember, "People who picked what you picked also grabbed this," and hand it to you without knowing a single fact about what's inside.

cards.title: Same movie, two different suggestions
cards.body: You just finished a space adventure movie. A content-based engine scans its tags — sci-fi, space, adventure — and suggests another movie with those same tags. A collaborative engine instead looks at everyone else who watched that movie, sees what THEY watched next, and suggests that — even a comedy, if enough similar viewers went on to watch one.

cards.title: Collaborative filtering doesn't need to understand anything
cards.body: You might assume any recommender must first "understand" what a movie is about. Collaborative filtering never looks at a single attribute — no genre, no actor, no plot. It works purely off patterns in who liked what, and it can still make great suggestions.

cards.title: Content-Based vs. Collaborative
cards.body: Two different signals, two different engines:

exercises.prompt: A bookstore app recommends a mystery novel because it shares three keyword tags — "detective", "noir", "twist ending" — with a book you rated five stars. Which approach is this?

options.text: Content-based filtering, because it's comparing item attributes/tags
Collaborative filtering, because it's comparing users
Neither — this requires knowing every other user's rating

exercises.explain: Right! Matching tags/attributes between items, with no reference to other users, is content-based filtering.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Collaborative filtering needs to know an item's genre, category, or other attributes to recommend it.
exercises.explain: False! Collaborative filtering only needs behavior data — who interacted with what — never the item's own attributes.

exercises.sentence: Content-based filtering compares item ___, while collaborative filtering compares user behavior.
exercises.explain: Content-based filtering is all about comparing the item's own attributes/features to what you already liked.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is an example of collaborative filtering?

options.text: A music app notices 10,000 listeners with tastes like yours all streamed a new artist this week, so it suggests that artist to you
A shopping app recommends hiking boots because you're viewing a tent tagged "outdoor gear"
A news app recommends articles tagged with the same topic as one you just read

exercises.explain: Correct! Recommending based on what similar listeners did — without ever checking the artist's genre — is collaborative filtering in action.

exercises.prompt: A brand-new podcast app has thousands of users but has never tagged any podcast with a genre or topic — there's no metadata at all, just play history. Which approach could still work well here, and why?

options.text: Collaborative filtering, because it only needs behavior data (who played what) — it doesn't require any item attributes to function
Content-based filtering, because it works fine with zero item attributes
Neither approach can work without labeled genre data
Content-based filtering, because user behavior data is irrelevant to it

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since collaborative filtering never looks at item attributes in the first place, missing metadata doesn't stop it — as long as there's enough behavior data connecting users to items.
